mappings.rationaleAI: Philosophy addressing fundamental questions maps to diverse conceptions (A.1), methods (A.2), and divisions including metaphysics (B.1), epistemology (B.3), philosophy of mind (B.4), and ethics (B.6).
Methodology of philosophical inquiry maps to diverse views of methods (A.2), criteria of meaning and truth (A.4), and the relation of philosophy of nature to science (B.2.b).
Core metaphysical problems including substance, causation, modality, and personal identity map to the nature and scope of metaphysics (B.1.a) and problems in metaphysics (B.1.c).
Major ethical frameworks map to types of ethics (B.6.a) and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Authority, democracy, and social contract theory map to nature and scope (B.7.a) and history (B.7.c) of political philosophy.
Art, beauty, taste, and aesthetic experience map to the nature of aesthetics (B.8.a) and problems in aesthetics (B.8.c).
Scientific reasoning and demarcation map to philosophy of nature (B.2), its relation to science (B.2.b), and philosophy of science (B.13).
Darwinian evolution, function, and species concepts map directly to philosophy of biology (B.2.e).
Space and time, irreversibility, and quantum mechanics interpretation map directly to philosophy of physics (B.2.d).
Arguments about God's existence and faith-reason relations map to philosophy of religion (B.14) and metaphysics-theology relation (B.1.b).
Legal systems, natural law, and positivism map to philosophy of law (B.15).
Mind-body relationship, consciousness, and competing frameworks map to philosophy of mind (B.4.a) and its problems (B.4.c).
Knowledge, belief, and justification map to epistemology's nature (B.3.a) and problems (B.3.c).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationaleAI: Language meaning, reference, and speech acts map to philosophy of language (B.9).
Propositional logic, predicate logic, and modal logic map to philosophy of logic (B.10).
The hard problem, neural correlates, and qualia map to problems in philosophy of mind (B.4.c).
Determinism, compatibilism, and moral responsibility span philosophy of mind (B.4.c), philosophical anthropology (B.5.c), and ethics (B.6.c).
Nature of reality maps to metaphysics (B.1) and its problems (B.1.c), and epistemological problems (B.3.c).
Causation theories map to problems in metaphysics (B.1.c) and basic aspects of the natural order (B.2.c).
Nature of time maps to metaphysical problems (B.1.c), natural order (B.2.c), and philosophy of physics (B.2.d).
Utilitarian theory maps to types of ethics (B.6.a) and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Virtue frameworks map to types of ethics (B.6.a) and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Environmental values and principles represent applied problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Ethical treatment of animals and moral status theories map to problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Healthcare moral questions map to ethics' relation to other knowledge (B.6.b) and problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Philosophical analysis of evil maps to problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Love's varieties and paradoxes connect to philosophical anthropology (B.5.c) and ethics (B.6.c).
Beauty as universal value maps to aesthetics (B.8) and its problems (B.8.c).
Meaning of life maps to philosophical anthropology (B.5.c) and ethics (B.6.c).
Conscience as moral faculty maps to problems in ethics (B.6.c).
Toleration in modern political philosophy maps to history of political philosophy (B.7.c).
Objectivity across domains maps to epistemological problems (B.3.c) and philosophy of nature's relation to science (B.2.b).
Logical analysis and conceptual clarification represent a distinctive conception (A.1) and method (A.2) of philosophy.
Continental philosophy represents a distinct conception (A.1), alternative methods (A.2), and connects to metaphysics (B.1) and philosophical anthropology (B.5).
Hermeneutic theory maps to philosophical methods (A.2), criteria of meaning and truth (A.4), and epistemological problems (B.3.c).
Gender oppression and intersectional knowledge connect to epistemology (B.3.c), philosophical anthropology (B.5.c), ethics (B.6.c), and political philosophy (B.7).
Arguments about divine existence map to metaphysics-theology relation (B.1.b) and philosophy of religion (B.14).
Atheism as philosophical position maps to philosophy of religion (B.14).
Agnosticism as epistemological position maps to epistemological problems (B.3.c) and philosophy of religion (B.14).
Humanism maps to philosophical anthropology (B.5) and philosophy of religion (B.14).
Secularism maps to political philosophy (B.7) and philosophy of religion (B.14).
Information as foundational concept connects to epistemological problems (B.3.c) and philosophy of language (B.9).
Mathematical infinity connects to philosophy of mathematics (B.11).
Nothing and vacuum map to metaphysical problems (B.1.c) and philosophy of physics (B.2.d).
Imagination as cognitive power maps to philosophy of mind problems (B.4.c) and aesthetics problems (B.8.c).
Nature of thought maps to philosophy of mind (B.4) and its problems (B.4.c).
Demarcation problem connects to philosophy of nature's relation to science (B.2.b) and philosophy of science (B.13).
